summary: Turning now to the new issues desk. Here is the prior day investment grade bond market report.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between three distinct regimes. First, we have the exhaustion of the AI momentum trade. When the leaders of a bull market start to stumble on earnings quality, the entire factor structure of the equity market tends to reset. Second, we have the "Warsh effect" at the Fed.

the broader picture now.

The single most important variable to watch today is the crude oil price reaction to the naval blockade news. If we see a sustained breakout there, expect the bear flattener in Treasuries to accelerate as the market prices in a Fed that is boxed in by stagflationary risks.

That’s the briefing for this morning. I’ll see you back here tomorrow.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between the A.I. growth narrative and a 1970s-style supply shock. We have a situation where the equity market is relying on technical support from buybacks, while the bond market is pricing in a structural shift in the inflation floor.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between the A.I. growth narrative and a structural supply shock in duration. We have a situation where the equity market is relying on technical support and buybacks, while the bond market is pricing in a structural shift in the inflation floor.
Before we close, a look at some research that came across the desk.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between the optimism of a geopolitical truce and the reality of a sticky inflation floor. We have a situation where the equity market is celebrating the removal of a tail risk, while the bond market is realizing that the removal of that risk also removes the Fed’s path to easing.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between the optimism of a resilient labor market and the reality of a deteriorating geopolitical floor. We have a situation where the equity market is celebrating the removal of recession risk, while the bond market is realizing that the removal of that risk—combined with the Hormuz escalation—effectively traps the Fed.

the broader picture now.

Watch the relationship between the Swiss Franc and luxury demand signals like the Breitling layoffs. It’s a niche data point, but it’s often a leading indicator for the global "wealth effect" trade. If the top-end consumer is retrenching while energy costs are rising for everyone else, the "soft landing" narrative starts to look very thin.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between the optimism of a resilient equity earnings cycle and the reality of a deteriorating geopolitical floor. We have a situation where equity analysts are raising targets based on productivity gains, while the bond market is realizing that the removal of the geopolitical truce also removes the Fed’s path to easing.

The report also flags a cooling in the global consumer. Global auto sales fell nearly three percent in April. While some of this is a normalization from a record March, the analysts warn that high fuel prices are starting to eat into discretionary big-ticket spending. This is particularly visible in the Euro area, where the labor market is finally showing real cracks.

the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is a collision between the A.I.-driven equity melt-up and the reality of a Fed chair who might actually let the market find its own clearing price for risk. We have a situation where equity analysts are raising targets based on SpaceX-era productivity, while the bond market is realizing that the "Fed put" has a much higher strike price under Warsh.
